parentQuestion: The Na‚Āļ/K‚Āļ ATPase pump moves ___ Na‚Āļ ions out and ___ K‚Āļ ions into the cell per cycle.

scaffolds.q: What is the Na‚Āļ/K‚Āļ ATPase pump?
options: An active transporter that moves sodium out and potassium into cells
scaffolds.explain: The Na‚Āļ/K‚Āļ ATPase PUMP is an ACTIVE TRANSPORTER that moves SODIUM OUT and POTASSIUM INTO cells using ATP.

wrongExplains.0: Passive channels don't use ATP; this pump requires it.
wrongExplains.2: Ribosomes make proteins; this is a membrane pump.
wrongExplains.3: Receptors bind signals; this pump moves ions.

options: Movement against a concentration gradient using ATP
scaffolds.explain: ACTIVE TRANSPORT moves substances AGAINST their concentration gradient, requiring ATP energy to do 'uphill' work.

wrongExplains.0: Movement down gradient is passive; active goes against it.
wrongExplains.2: Diffusion is passive; active transport uses energy.
wrongExplains.3: Osmosis is passive water movement; active transport uses ATP.

scaffolds.explain: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the cell's ENERGY CURRENCY. It provides energy for active transport and other processes.

wrongExplains.0: DNA stores genetic information; ATP stores energy.
wrongExplains.2: Phospholipids form membranes; ATP provides energy.
wrongExplains.3: Proteins do work; ATP provides the energy for that work.

scaffolds.q: What ions does the Na‚Āļ/K‚Āļ pump move?
scaffolds.explain: The Na‚Āļ/K‚Āļ pump moves SODIUM (Na‚Āļ) OUT and POTASSIUM (K‚Āļ) IN. This maintains the ionic gradient essential for cell function.

wrongExplains.0: Different pumps handle calcium; this one moves Na‚Āļ and K‚Āļ.
wrongExplains.2: Other transporters handle H‚Āļ; this pump moves Na‚Āļ and K‚Āļ.
wrongExplains.3: This pump specifically moves sodium and potassium.

scaffolds.q: In which direction does the pump move sodium?
scaffolds.explain: The pump moves SODIUM OUT of the cell. This maintains low intracellular sodium concentration.

wrongExplains.0: Sodium goes out; potassium comes in.
wrongExplains.2: The pump is directional ‚ÄĒ Na‚Āļ out, K‚Āļ in.
wrongExplains.3: The pump definitely moves sodium ‚ÄĒ three ions out per cycle.

scaffolds.q: In which direction does the pump move potassium?
scaffolds.explain: The pump moves POTASSIUM INTO the cell. This maintains high intracellular potassium concentration.

wrongExplains.0: Potassium goes in; sodium goes out.
wrongExplains.2: The pump is directional ‚ÄĒ K‚Āļ in, Na‚Āļ out.
wrongExplains.3: The pump definitely moves potassium ‚ÄĒ two ions in per cycle.

scaffolds.q: Why is the Na‚Āļ/K‚Āļ pump important for cells?
options: It maintains the electrochemical gradient needed for nerve impulses and cell function
scaffolds.explain: The pump maintains the ELECTROCHEMICAL GRADIENT needed for nerve impulses, muscle contraction, and secondary active transport.

wrongExplains.0: The pump uses ATP; mitochondria produce it.
wrongExplains.2: Ribosomes synthesize proteins; the pump moves ions.
wrongExplains.3: DNA stores genetic information; the pump maintains ion gradients.

scaffolds.q: How many ions does the Na‚Āļ/K‚Āļ pump move per cycle?
scaffolds.explain: The pump moves 3 Na‚Āļ OUT and 2 K‚Āļ IN per ATP hydrolyzed. This unequal exchange makes the pump ELECTROGENIC.

wrongExplains.2: It's 3 Na‚Āļ out, 2 K‚Āļ in ‚ÄĒ not reversed.
wrongExplains.3: The exchange is unequal (3 out, 2 in), making it electrogenic.

scaffolds.q: The Na‚Āļ/K‚Āļ ATPase pump moves ___ Na‚Āļ ions out and ___ K‚Āļ ions into the cell per cycle?
scaffolds.explain: The pump moves 3 Na‚Āļ OUT and 2 K‚Āļ IN per cycle. This 3:2 ratio creates a net positive charge movement out of the cell.

wrongExplains.0: The ratio is 3 Na‚Āļ out, 2 K‚Āļ in ‚ÄĒ not reversed.
